questions.question:
A circularly linked list is used to represent a Queue. A single variable p is used to access the Queue. To which node should p point such that both the operations enQueue and deQueue can be performed in constant time?

A. rear node
B. front node
C. not possible with a single pointer
D. node next to front
questions.to_find: Which node should the single pointer p point to for O(1) enQueue and deQueue on a circular linked list?

understand.plain: We have a circular singly linked list used as a Queue. We only have one pointer to navigate the whole list. We need to pick what that pointer points to so that both adding (enQueue at rear) and removing (deQueue from front) are O(1) — no traversal needed.

keywords.explain: A linked list where the last node's next pointer points back to the first node, forming a circle.
enQueue adds an element at the rear; deQueue removes an element from the front of the queue.
keywords.example: enQueue(5): new node added after current rear. deQueue: front node removed.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='160' viewBox='0 0 480 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='50' width='80' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Front</text><rect x='160' y='50' width='80' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>...</text><rect x='300' y='50' width='80' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='340' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Rear</text><line x1='100' y1='75' x2='160' y2='75' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='240' y1='75' x2='300' y2='75' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><path d='M380 100 Q420 140 60 140 Q20 140 20 100' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='340' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>p points here</text><line x1='340' y1='40' x2='340' y2='50' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr2)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker><marker id='arr2' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z' fill='#D85A30'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A circular linked list with nodes Front ... Rear. The pointer p points to the Rear node. A curved arrow from Rear goes back to Front, completing the circle.

given.aim: Find which node the single pointer should point to for O(1) enQueue and deQueue on a circular linked list.

terms.meaning: If p points to rear, then p->next is the front node since the list is circular.
terms.example: Rear->next = Front in a circular list.
terms.connects: Accessing front is p->next in O(1); accessing rear is p itself in O(1).

terms.meaning: Insert a new node after the rear node and update rear.
terms.example: newNode->next = p->next (old front); p->next = newNode; p = newNode.
terms.connects: O(1) since rear is directly accessible via p.

terms.meaning: Remove the front node, which is p->next in the circular list when p = rear.
terms.connects: O(1) since front = p->next, no traversal needed.

given.plan: If p points to rear: front = p->next (O(1) access for deQueue). To enQueue, insert after rear and move p to the new node in O(1). If p pointed to front instead, we would need O(n) traversal to reach rear for enQueue.

steps.title: Analyse enQueue with p = rear
steps.formula_raw: newNode->next = p->next; p->next = newNode; p = newNode;
steps.apply: Insert new node after current rear (p). Old front is still reachable as newNode->next. Then advance p to newNode (new rear). All O(1) — no traversal.
steps.note: No traversal is needed since p already points to the insertion point.

steps.title: Analyse deQueue with p = rear
steps.apply: Front is p->next (direct access). Skip it by setting p->next = front->next, then free the old front. All O(1).
steps.note: If p pointed to front instead, deQueue would be O(1) but enQueue would need O(n) traversal to reach rear.

solution.result: p should point to the rear node. Answer: A. rear node

questions.formula_note: In a circular linked list, pointing to the rear gives O(1) access to both rear (for insertion) and front (via rear->next, for deletion), enabling a constant-time queue.

questions.question:
Suppose each set is represented as a linked list with elements in arbitrary order. Which of the operations among union, intersection, membership, cardinality will be the slowest?

A. union only
B. intersection, membership
C. membership, cardinality
D. union, intersection
questions.to_find: Which set operations are the slowest when sets are represented as unsorted linked lists?

understand.plain: Sets are stored as linked lists in arbitrary (unsorted) order. We compare the time complexity of four operations: union (combine two sets), intersection (common elements), membership (is x in the set?), and cardinality (count elements). Membership and cardinality each need only one scan of one list. Union and intersection require comparing every element of one list against all elements of the other, making them much slower.

keywords.explain: Check if an element belongs to a set by scanning through the list until found or list ends.
Union combines all unique elements of two sets; intersection finds common elements. Both require comparing every element of one set against all elements of the other.
keywords.example: A={1,2,3}, B={2,3,4}: for each of 3 elements in A, scan all 3 elements in B — 9 comparisons = O(n^2).

understand.visual_alt: Complexity table: membership O(n), cardinality O(n), union O(n^2), intersection O(n^2). Union and intersection are the slowest.

given.aim: Determine which set operations have the highest time complexity on unordered linked-list representations of sets.

terms.meaning: Scan one list of size n to find the element.
terms.example: Is x in A? Compare x with each element of A — at most n comparisons.

terms.meaning: Count the number of nodes in the list by one full traversal.
For each element in set A (size m), perform a membership check in set B (size n). Total comparisons: m x n = O(n^2) for equal-sized sets.
terms.example: |A|=|B|=n: n elements each checked against n others = n^2 operations.
terms.connects: Quadratic time — the slowest among the four operations.

given.plan: Compute the asymptotic complexity of each operation on unordered linked lists and identify the most expensive ones.

steps.title: Complexity of membership and cardinality
steps.apply: Membership: scan list once to find element — O(n). Cardinality: count all nodes by one traversal — O(n). Both are linear operations.
steps.note: These are straightforward single-pass operations on one list.

steps.title: Complexity of union and intersection
steps.apply: Union: for each element in A, scan all of B to check for duplicates before appending. Intersection: for each element in A, scan all of B to check membership. If |A|=|B|=n, both require O(n^2) comparisons. No sorting or hashing is available on arbitrary-order linked lists.
steps.note: Union and intersection are the slowest among the four operations.

solution.result: Union and intersection are the slowest at O(n^2). Answer: D. union, intersection

questions.formula_note: Set union and intersection on unordered linked lists require O(n^2) pairwise membership checks, making them slower than O(n) membership and cardinality operations.

questions.to_find: The contents of the linked list after rearrange() is applied to 1->2->3->4->5->6->7.

understand.plain: The function rearrange() uses two pointers p and q to traverse adjacent pairs of nodes and swaps their values. Starting from the head, it swaps (1,2), then (3,4), then (5,6), and leaves node 7 unchanged because there is no pair for it. Only values are exchanged, not node pointers.

keywords.explain: The function swaps the 'value' field of two adjacent nodes using a temp variable, leaving the node pointers (next) unchanged.
keywords.example: Nodes [1]->[2] become [2]->[1] after one swap iteration.

keywords.explain: p and q always point to two consecutive nodes. After each swap, both advance by two positions to the next pair.
keywords.example: Iteration 1: p=node1, q=node2. After swap and advance: p=node3, q=node4.

y='123' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>5</text><rect x='296' y='100' width='40' height='34' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='316' y='123' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>7</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Before: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7. After pairwise swaps: 2 1 4 3 6 5 7. Node 7 is unchanged because it has no pair.

given.aim: Trace the rearrange function on 1->2->3->4->5->6->7 and determine the resulting list.

terms.meaning: p points to the first node of a pair, q points to the second (q = p->next). The swap operates on p->value and q->value.
terms.connects: Each loop iteration performs one pairwise value swap.

terms.meaning: After each swap: p = q->next (skip to next pair start), q = p ? p->next : 0.
terms.example: After processing pair (node1, node2): p = node3, q = node4.
terms.connects: Each iteration covers exactly two nodes.

terms.meaning: 7 elements form 3 complete pairs (1,2),(3,4),(5,6) and one leftover element (7).
terms.example: After 3 swaps: p = node7, q = NULL, loop ends without swapping node7.
terms.connects: The 7th element stays in its original relative position.

given.plan: Trace the while loop iteration by iteration, applying value swaps and advancing the pointers p and q, to find the final order.

steps.title: Iteration 1: swap nodes at positions 1 and 2
steps.apply: p=node(1), q=node(2). Swap values: node1 gets 2, node2 gets 1. Advance: p=node(3), q=node(4). List: 2,1,3,4,5,6,7.
steps.note: Only values are swapped, node pointers (next) remain unchanged.

steps.title: Iteration 2: swap nodes at positions 3 and 4
steps.apply: p=node(3) value=3, q=node(4) value=4. Swap: node3 gets 4, node4 gets 3. Advance: p=node(5), q=node(6). List: 2,1,4,3,5,6,7.

steps.title: Iteration 3: swap nodes at positions 5 and 6
steps.apply: p=node(5) value=5, q=node(6) value=6. Swap: node5 gets 6, node6 gets 5. Advance: p=node(7), q=node(7)->next=NULL. Loop terminates (q is NULL). Final list: 2,1,4,3,6,5,7.
steps.note: Node 7 has no partner so it is never swapped.

solution.result: The list after execution: 2, 1, 4, 3, 6, 5, 7. Answer: B. 2, 1, 4, 3, 6, 5, 7

questions.formula_note: The function performs pairwise adjacent swap of integer values (not pointers) in a singly linked list, reversing each consecutive pair.

questions.answer: A. q->next = NULL; p->next = head; head = p;
questions.to_find: The correct three-line code to complete move_to_front() so that the last node becomes the new head.

understand.plain: After the while loop, p points to the last node and q points to the second-to-last node. To move the last node to the front we need three steps: (1) cut p from the end by setting q->next = NULL, (2) link p to the old head by setting p->next = head, (3) update head to p. This must be done in this exact order so the old head value is not lost before being assigned to p->next.

keywords.explain: q lags one step behind p throughout the traversal. When p reaches the last node, q is at the second-to-last node.
Detach the last node from its current position and insert it before the current head to make it the new first node.

given.aim: Complete the move_to_front function so that the last node becomes the new head of the singly-linked list.

terms.meaning: After the while loop, p points to the last node (p->next == NULL).
terms.connects: p must become the new head.

terms.meaning: q is the node just before p. Setting q->next = NULL disconnects p from the rest of the list.
terms.example: q = node(3); q->next = NULL makes the list 1->2->3->NULL (p is now detached).
terms.connects: Must be done first to avoid a dangling link at the end.

terms.meaning: We must save the old head value (used as p->next) before overwriting head with p. Hence: p->next = head; then head = p.
terms.example: p->next = head (= node1); head = p (= node4). Final: 4->1->2->3->NULL.
terms.connects: Swapping the order (head=p first) would create a self-loop: p->next = p.

given.plan: Three pointer operations in this exact order: (1) q->next = NULL to cut p off the end, (2) p->next = head to link p to the old head, (3) head = p to make p the new head.

steps.title: Step q->next = NULL
steps.apply: Disconnects the last node p from the list. The list is now: head->...->q->NULL, and p is a separate node with p->next still pointing to NULL.
steps.note: This prevents a cycle at the tail after p is moved.

steps.title: Step p->next = head
steps.apply: Links the last node p to point to the current (old) head. Now p->next = node(1). This must happen before head is updated, otherwise the old head reference is lost.
steps.note: If we set head = p first, then p->next = head would be p->next = p (self-loop) — incorrect.

steps.title: Step head = p
steps.apply: Updates head to point to p (the former last node). Final list: p->node(1)->node(2)->...->q->NULL. For example with input 1->2->3->4: result is 4->1->2->3->NULL.
steps.note: The function returns head which now points to the moved node.

solution.result: Correct code: q->next = NULL; p->next = head; head = p; Answer: A. q->next = NULL; p->next = head; head = p;

questions.formula_note: Moving the last node to the front of a linked list requires three pointer updates in the correct order: cut last from tail (q->next=NULL), link last to old head (p->next=head), update head (head=p).
